philosophy.title: Marketing (anglicismo ocasionalmente adaptado ao português como mercadologia) é o ato de adquirir, s
philosophy.description: Marketing (anglicismo ocasionalmente adaptado ao português como mercadologia) é o ato de adquirir, satisfazer e reter clientes. É um dos principais componentes da administração de empresas e do comércio. O marketing é geralmente conduzido pelo vendedor, tipicamente um varejista ou fabricante. Produtos podem ser comercializados para outras empresas (marketing B2B) ou diretamente aos consumidores (B2C). Às vezes, tarefas são contratadas para empresas especializadas em marketing, como uma agência de mídia, pesquisa de mercado ou agência de publicidade. Às vezes, uma associação comercial ou agência governamental faz publicidade em nome de toda uma indústria ou localidade, frequentemente um tipo específico de alimento, alimento de uma área específica, ou uma cidade ou região como destino turístico. Orientações de mercado são filosofias concernentes aos fatores que devem entrar no planejamento de mercado. O mix de marketing, que delineia as especificidades do produto e como ele será vendido, incluindo os canais que serão usados para divulgar o produto, é afetado pelo ambiente ao redor do produto, os resultados da pesquisa de marketing e pesquisa de mercado, e as características do mercado-alvo do produto. Uma vez que esses fatores são determinados, os profissionais de marketing devem então decidir quais métodos de promoção do produto, incluindo o uso de cupons e outros incentivos de preço.

philosophy.title: Philip Kotler (born May 27, 1931) is an American marketing author, consultant, and professor emeritu
philosophy.description: Philip Kotler (born May 27, 1931) is an American marketing author, consultant, and professor emeritus. He was the S. C. Johnson & Son Distinguished Professor of International Marketing at the Kellogg School of Management at Northwestern University (1962–2018). He is known for popularizing the concept of the marketing mix. He is the author of over 80 books, including Marketing Management, Principles of Marketing, Kotler on Marketing, Marketing Insights from A to Z, Marketing 4.0, Marketing Places, Marketing of Nations, Chaotics, Market Your Way to Growth, Winning Global Markets, Strategic Marketing for Health Care Organizations, Social Marketing, Social Media Marketing, My Adventures in Marketing, Up and Out of Poverty, and Winning at Innovation. Kotler has described strategic marketing as "the link between society's needs and its pattern of industrial response." Kotler helped create the field of social marketing that focuses on helping individuals and groups modify their behaviors toward healthier and safer lifestyles. He also created the concept of "demarketing" to aid in the task of reducing the level of demand. He developed the concepts of "prosumers," "atmospherics," and "societal marketing." He is regarded as "The Father of Modern Marketing" by many scholars. Kotler has served as a consultant to major corporations, including IBM, General Electric, AT&T, Honeywell, Merck, and Bank of America. He has also advised national governments on industrial competitiveness and place marketing strategies, particularly in Asia and Europe. His consulting work and lectures delivered in more than 60 countries have helped global organizations integrate social responsibility into business strategy. Kotler's latest work focuses on economic justice and the shortcomings of capitalism. He published Confronting Capitalism: Real Solutions for a Troubled Economic System in 2015, Democracy in Decline: Rebuilding its Future in 2016, "Advancing the Common Good" in 2019, and Brand Activism: From Purpose to Action in 2018.

philosophy.title: # The Evolution of Modern Marketing: From Digital Integration to Human-to-Human (H2H) Connections ##
philosophy.description: # The Evolution of Modern Marketing: From Digital Integration to Human-to-Human (H2H) Connections ## Executive Summary The landscape of global marketing is undergoing a fundamental transformation, shifting from vertical, exclusive, and individual structures to a model that is horizontal, inclusive, and social. This document synthesizes the strategic frameworks developed by Philip Kotler and his collaborators, primarily focusing on the transition from traditional marketing to **Marketing 4.0** and the emerging **Marketing H2H (Human-to-Human)** concept. The core of this evolution is the empowerment of the connected consumer. In an era defined by digital transparency and rapid technological disruption, brands can no longer rely on unilateral messaging. Success now depends on a brand’s ability to humanize itself, foster genuine connections, and navigate the "5 As" customer journey: Assimilation, Attraction, Asking, Action, and Apology (Advocacy). The ultimate objective of modern marketing is no longer just a transaction, but the cultivation of loyal brand advocates. --- ## 1. The Strategic Shift in Power and Influence The rise of the internet and connectivity has dismantled traditional power structures. Marketing has evolved through several distinct stages, moving toward a more empathetic, social approach. ### From Vertical to Horizontal Historically, marketing was vertical, where companies (the "hunters") targeted consumers (the "prey") through one-way communication. Today, influence is horizontal. Consumers trust their social circles—friends, family, and online peers—more than they trust corporate advertising or experts. ### The Influence of the JMN Subcultures Three specific demographic segments, collectively known as **JMN**, act as the most influential drivers in the digital economy: * **Youth (Jovens):** Early adopters and trendsetters who define "cool" and push for social change. * **Women (Mulheres):** The "household CFOs" and "information collectors" who perform holistic evaluations and manage complex family purchasing decisions. * **Netizens (Cidadãos da Internet):** Online "evangelists" and "connectors" who actively create content, rate products, and build the "hearts" of brand communities. ### The Paradoxes of Connectivity Connectivity introduces three primary paradoxes that marketers must manage: 1. **Online vs. Offline Interaction:** Brands must integrate digital speed with the "high-touch" of physical presence. 2. **Informed vs. Distracted Consumer:** Consumers have more information than ever but have shorter attention spans (averaging 8 seconds), making them dependent on the opinions of others. 3. **Negative vs. Positive Advocacy:** In a transparent world, a brand with a strong DNA will naturally attract "haters." These negative voices often trigger "lovers" to defend the brand, creating a dynamic social dialogue. --- ## 2. The New Customer Journey: The 5 As The traditional AIDA (Attention, Interest, Desire, Action) funnel has been replaced by a more flexible, social model known as the **5 As**. This model accounts for the fact that buying is now a social process. | Stage | Description | Key Impression | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Assimilation (A1)** | Consumers are passively exposed to a long list of brands via ads or past experience. | "I know" | | **Attraction (A2)** | Consumers process messages and become attracted to a "shortlist" of memorable brands. | "I like" | | **Asking (A3)** | Driven by curiosity, consumers research, ask friends for advice, and check online reviews. | "I am convinced" | | **Action (A4)** | The consumer makes the purchase and interacts through consumption and post-sale service. | "I am buying" | | **Apology (A5)** | Consumers develop loyalty and spontaneously recommend or defend the brand to others. | "I recommend" | ### The Zone of Influence (POE) A consumer’s progress through the 5 As is influenced by the **Zone POE**: * **External (P):** Influence from brand communications (ads, sales teams). * **Others (O):** Influence from social circles, family, and online communities (the most powerful source). * **Personal (E):** Influence from the consumer’s own past experiences and preferences. --- ## 3. Redefining the Marketing Mix: From 4 Ps to 4 Cs In the digital economy, the traditional 4 Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion) must be re-envisioned to allow for greater customer participation. | Traditional 4 Ps | Digital 4 Cs | Strategic Definition | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Product** | **Co-creation** | Involving customers in the design and personalization of products. | | **Price** | **Currency** | Utilizing dynamic pricing that fluctuates based on market demand and data. | | **Place** | **Communal Activation** | Leveraging peer-to-peer distribution (e.g., Airbnb, Uber) and local access. | | **Promotion** | **Conversation** | Shifting from one-way broadcasting to two-way social dialogue and reviews. | *Note: Kotler also highlights an evolution to **7 Ps**, adding **Service**, **Brand**, and **Incentive** to the original mix to ensure competitiveness in a service-oriented market.* --- ## 4. Marketing H2H: Human-to-Human The latest evolution, **Marketing H2H**, proposes a movement to humanize marketing practices entirely. It focuses on empathy, ethics, and a sense of community. * **Design Thinking:** H2H incorporates design as a fundamental marketing tool for conceptualizing services and distribution. * **Service Integration:** It emphasizes services that accompany a product, focusing on the customer’s long-term needs (e.g., recycling printer cartridges rather than just selling new ones). * **Employee Centrality:** H2H recognizes that unhappy employees cannot provide a high-quality human experience to customers. --- ## 5. Important Quotes and Context > **"A brand is a promise; it works strongly and well if it fulfills what it promises, or fails if it doesn't."** *Context: Philip Kotler discussing the fundamental nature of branding in the 21st century.* > **"The company that relates to the customer only through technology is doomed to failure."** *Context: Kotler on the necessity of balancing "High-Tech" with "High-Touch" (human contact).* > **"Marketing H2H... is the new way of working in marketing: organizations so focused on their essence and role in the community that they can continuously adapt to their ecosystem's needs."** *Context: From the launch of Kotler’s "Marketing H2H" in Brazil, highlighting the need for strategic coherence.* > **"The ultimate goal of Marketing 4.0 is to drive consumers from assimilation to apology."** *Context: Explaining that the pinnacle of marketing success is no longer the sale, but creating a brand advocate.* --- ## 6. Actionable Insights ### Foster Brand Advocacy Do not just aim for a transaction. Map the customer journey to identify the "UAU!" (WOW!) moments that can turn a buyer into a defender. Focus on the **Apology** (Advocacy) stage as the primary metric of success. ### Engage the JMN Segments * **For Youth:** Use creative content and digital trends to gain "mind share." * **For Women:** Provide detailed, holistic information and quality comparisons to win "market share." * **For Netizens:** Facilitate online communities and authentic storytelling to win "heart share." ### Balance Tech and Touch Use Artificial Intelligence (IA) and data science to improve productivity and speed, but ensure these tools are partners to human empathy. AI can generate images and text, but it must be combined with human contact to avoid a "mechanical" sales vision. ### Transition to the 4 Cs * **Co-create:** Invite customers to help develop your next product. * **Currency:** Implement dynamic pricing strategies based on big data. * **Communal Activation:** Find ways to bring your product closer to the community for near-instant access. * **Conversation:** Encourage and respond to social media reviews, even the negative ones, to spark authentic dialogue. ### Adopt an H2H Mindset Ensure your CEO, CFO, and CMO are aligned on a humanistic view. This includes treating employees with empathy, as their satisfaction directly impacts the customer experience and the brand’s sustainability.

methodologies.title: Philip Kotler (Chicago, 27 de maio de 1931) é um professor universitário estadunidense
methodologies.description: Philip Kotler (Chicago, 27 de maio de 1931) é um professor universitário estadunidense. Distinto professor S.C. Johnson & Son de Marketing Internacional na Kellogg School of Management na Universidade Northwestern. Ele obteve seu mestrado na Universidade de Chicago e seu Ph.D. no Instituto Tecnológico de Massachusetts (MIT), ambos em economia. Ele fez pós-doutorado em matemática em Harvard e em ciências comportamentais na Universidade de Chicago. Foi selecionado em 2005 como o quarto maior guru de negócios pelo Financial Times (atrás de Jack Welch, Bill Gates e Peter Drucker) e foi considerado pelo Management Centre Europe "o maior dos especialistas na prática do marketing." Em 2008, o Wall Street Journal o listou como a sexta pessoa mais influente no mundo dos negócios. Kotler já prestou consultoria a grandes empresas, inclusive IBM, Michelin, Bank of America, Merck, General Electric, Honeywell e Motorola - nas áreas de estratégia de marketing, planejamento e organização e marketing internacional. Ele apresenta seminários em vários grandes centros ao redor do mundo sobre os últimos desenvolvimentos do marketing. Considerado um dos mercadólogos mais importante da história.

methodologies.description: Marketing (anglicismo ocasionalmente adaptado ao português como mercadologia) é o ato de adquirir, satisfazer e reter clientes. É um dos principais componentes da administração de empresas e do comércio. O marketing é geralmente conduzido pelo vendedor, tipicamente um varejista ou fabricante. Produtos podem ser comercializados para outras empresas (marketing B2B) ou diretamente aos consumidores (B2C). Às vezes, tarefas são contratadas para empresas especializadas em marketing, como uma agência de mídia, pesquisa de mercado ou agência de publicidade. Às vezes, uma associação comercial ou agência governamental faz publicidade em nome de toda uma indústria ou localidade, frequentemente um tipo específico de alimento, alimento de uma área específica, ou uma cidade ou região como destino turístico. Orientações de mercado são filosofias concernentes aos fatores que devem entrar no planejamento de mercado. O mix de marketing, que delineia as especificidades do produto e como ele será vendido, incluindo os canais que serão usados para divulgar o produto, é afetado pelo ambiente ao redor do produto, os resultados da pesquisa de marketing e pesquisa de mercado, e as características do mercado-alvo do produto. Uma vez que esses fatores são determinados, os profissionais de marketing devem então decidir quais métodos de promoção do produto, incluindo o uso de cupons e outros incentivos de preço.

methodologies.title: Philip Kotler (born May 27, 1931) is an American marketing author, consultant, and professor emeritu
methodologies.description: Philip Kotler (born May 27, 1931) is an American marketing author, consultant, and professor emeritus. He was the S. C. Johnson & Son Distinguished Professor of International Marketing at the Kellogg School of Management at Northwestern University (1962–2018). He is known for popularizing the concept of the marketing mix. He is the author of over 80 books, including Marketing Management, Principles of Marketing, Kotler on Marketing, Marketing Insights from A to Z, Marketing 4.0, Marketing Places, Marketing of Nations, Chaotics, Market Your Way to Growth, Winning Global Markets, Strategic Marketing for Health Care Organizations, Social Marketing, Social Media Marketing, My Adventures in Marketing, Up and Out of Poverty, and Winning at Innovation. Kotler has described strategic marketing as "the link between society's needs and its pattern of industrial response." Kotler helped create the field of social marketing that focuses on helping individuals and groups modify their behaviors toward healthier and safer lifestyles. He also created the concept of "demarketing" to aid in the task of reducing the level of demand. He developed the concepts of "prosumers," "atmospherics," and "societal marketing." He is regarded as "The Father of Modern Marketing" by many scholars. Kotler has served as a consultant to major corporations, including IBM, General Electric, AT&T, Honeywell, Merck, and Bank of America. He has also advised national governments on industrial competitiveness and place marketing strategies, particularly in Asia and Europe. His consulting work and lectures delivered in more than 60 countries have helped global organizations integrate social responsibility into business strategy. Kotler's latest work focuses on economic justice and the shortcomings of capitalism. He published Confronting Capitalism: Real Solutions for a Troubled Economic System in 2015, Democracy in Decline: Rebuilding its Future in 2016, "Advancing the Common Good" in 2019, and Brand Activism: From Purpose to Action in 2018.

Philip Kotler (born May 27, 1931) is an American marketing author, consultant, and professor emeritu: Philip Kotler (born May 27, 1931) is an American marketing author, consultant, and professor emeritus. He was the S. C. Johnson & Son Distinguished Professor of International Marketing at the Kellogg School of Management at Northwestern University (1962–2018). He is known for popularizing the concept of the marketing mix. He is the author of over 80 books, including Marketing Management, Principles of Marketing, Kotler on Marketing, Marketing Insights from A to Z, Marketing 4.0, Marketing Places, Marketing of Nations, Chaotics, Market Your Way to Growth, Winning Global Markets, Strategic Marketing for Health Care Organizations, Social Marketing, Social Media Marketing, My Adventures in Marketing, Up and Out of Poverty, and Winning at Innovation. Kotler has described strategic marketing as "the link between society's needs and its pattern of industrial response." Kotler helped create the field of social marketing that focuses on helping individuals and groups modify their behaviors toward healthier and safer lifestyles. He also created the concept of "demarketing" to aid in the task of reducing the level of demand. He developed the concepts of "prosumers," "atmospherics," and "societal marketing." He is regarded as "The Father of Modern Marketing" by many scholars. Kotler has served as a consultant to major corporations, including IBM, General Electric, AT&T, Honeywell, Merck, and Bank of America. He has also advised national governments on industrial competitiveness and place marketing strategies, particularly in Asia and Europe. His consulting work and lectures delivered in more than 60 countries have helped global organizations integrate social responsibility into business strategy. Kotler's latest work focuses on economic justice and the shortcomings of capitalism. He published Confronting Capitalism: Real Solutions for a Troubled Economic System in 2015, Democracy in Decline: Rebuilding its Future in 2016, "Advancing the Common Good" in 2019, and Brand Activism: From Purpose to Action in 2018.

# The Evolution of Modern Marketing: From Digital Integration to Human-to-Human (H2H) Connections ##: # The Evolution of Modern Marketing: From Digital Integration to Human-to-Human (H2H) Connections ## Executive Summary The landscape of global marketing is undergoing a fundamental transformation, shifting from vertical, exclusive, and individual structures to a model that is horizontal, inclusive, and social. This document synthesizes the strategic frameworks developed by Philip Kotler and his collaborators, primarily focusing on the transition from traditional marketing to **Marketing 4.0** and the emerging **Marketing H2H (Human-to-Human)** concept. The core of this evolution is the empowerment of the connected consumer. In an era defined by digital transparency and rapid technological disruption, brands can no longer rely on unilateral messaging. Success now depends on a brand’s ability to humanize itself, foster genuine connections, and navigate the "5 As" customer journey: Assimilation, Attraction, Asking, Action, and Apology (Advocacy). The ultimate objective of modern marketing is no longer just a transaction, but the cultivation of loyal brand advocates. --- ## 1. The Strategic Shift in Power and Influence The rise of the internet and connectivity has dismantled traditional power structures. Marketing has evolved through several distinct stages, moving toward a more empathetic, social approach. ### From Vertical to Horizontal Historically, marketing was vertical, where companies (the "hunters") targeted consumers (the "prey") through one-way communication. Today, influence is horizontal. Consumers trust their social circles—friends, family, and online peers—more than they trust corporate advertising or experts. ### The Influence of the JMN Subcultures Three specific demographic segments, collectively known as **JMN**, act as the most influential drivers in the digital economy: * **Youth (Jovens):** Early adopters and trendsetters who define "cool" and push for social change. * **Women (Mulheres):** The "household CFOs" and "information collectors" who perform holistic evaluations and manage complex family purchasing decisions. * **Netizens (Cidadãos da Internet):** Online "evangelists" and "connectors" who actively create content, rate products, and build the "hearts" of brand communities. ### The Paradoxes of Connectivity Connectivity introduces three primary paradoxes that marketers must manage: 1. **Online vs. Offline Interaction:** Brands must integrate digital speed with the "high-touch" of physical presence. 2. **Informed vs. Distracted Consumer:** Consumers have more information than ever but have shorter attention spans (averaging 8 seconds), making them dependent on the opinions of others. 3. **Negative vs. Positive Advocacy:** In a transparent world, a brand with a strong DNA will naturally attract "haters." These negative voices often trigger "lovers" to defend the brand, creating a dynamic social dialogue. --- ## 2. The New Customer Journey: The 5 As The traditional AIDA (Attention, Interest, Desire, Action) funnel has been replaced by a more flexible, social model known as the **5 As**. This model accounts for the fact that buying is now a social process. | Stage | Description | Key Impression | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Assimilation (A1)** | Consumers are passively exposed to a long list of brands via ads or past experience. | "I know" | | **Attraction (A2)** | Consumers process messages and become attracted to a "shortlist" of memorable brands. | "I like" | | **Asking (A3)** | Driven by curiosity, consumers research, ask friends for advice, and check online reviews. | "I am convinced" | | **Action (A4)** | The consumer makes the purchase and interacts through consumption and post-sale service. | "I am buying" | | **Apology (A5)** | Consumers develop loyalty and spontaneously recommend or defend the brand to others. | "I recommend" | ### The Zone of Influence (POE) A consumer’s progress through the 5 As is influenced by the **Zone POE**: * **External (P):** Influence from brand communications (ads, sales teams). * **Others (O):** Influence from social circles, family, and online communities (the most powerful source). * **Personal (E):** Influence from the consumer’s own past experiences and preferences. --- ## 3. Redefining the Marketing Mix: From 4 Ps to 4 Cs In the digital economy, the traditional 4 Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion) must be re-envisioned to allow for greater customer participation. | Traditional 4 Ps | Digital 4 Cs | Strategic Definition | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Product** | **Co-creation** | Involving customers in the design and personalization of products. | | **Price** | **Currency** | Utilizing dynamic pricing that fluctuates based on market demand and data. | | **Place** | **Communal Activation** | Leveraging peer-to-peer distribution (e.g., Airbnb, Uber) and local access. | | **Promotion** | **Conversation** | Shifting from one-way broadcasting to two-way social dialogue and reviews. | *Note: Kotler also highlights an evolution to **7 Ps**, adding **Service**, **Brand**, and **Incentive** to the original mix to ensure competitiveness in a service-oriented market.* --- ## 4. Marketing H2H: Human-to-Human The latest evolution, **Marketing H2H**, proposes a movement to humanize marketing practices entirely. It focuses on empathy, ethics, and a sense of community. * **Design Thinking:** H2H incorporates design as a fundamental marketing tool for conceptualizing services and distribution. * **Service Integration:** It emphasizes services that accompany a product, focusing on the customer’s long-term needs (e.g., recycling printer cartridges rather than just selling new ones). * **Employee Centrality:** H2H recognizes that unhappy employees cannot provide a high-quality human experience to customers. --- ## 5. Important Quotes and Context > **"A brand is a promise; it works strongly and well if it fulfills what it promises, or fails if it doesn't."** *Context: Philip Kotler discussing the fundamental nature of branding in the 21st century.* > **"The company that relates to the customer only through technology is doomed to failure."** *Context: Kotler on the necessity of balancing "High-Tech" with "High-Touch" (human contact).* > **"Marketing H2H... is the new way of working in marketing: organizations so focused on their essence and role in the community that they can continuously adapt to their ecosystem's needs."** *Context: From the launch of Kotler’s "Marketing H2H" in Brazil, highlighting the need for strategic coherence.* > **"The ultimate goal of Marketing 4.0 is to drive consumers from assimilation to apology."** *Context: Explaining that the pinnacle of marketing success is no longer the sale, but creating a brand advocate.* --- ## 6. Actionable Insights ### Foster Brand Advocacy Do not just aim for a transaction. Map the customer journey to identify the "UAU!" (WOW!) moments that can turn a buyer into a defender. Focus on the **Apology** (Advocacy) stage as the primary metric of success. ### Engage the JMN Segments * **For Youth:** Use creative content and digital trends to gain "mind share." * **For Women:** Provide detailed, holistic information and quality comparisons to win "market share." * **For Netizens:** Facilitate online communities and authentic storytelling to win "heart share." ### Balance Tech and Touch Use Artificial Intelligence (IA) and data science to improve productivity and speed, but ensure these tools are partners to human empathy. AI can generate images and text, but it must be combined with human contact to avoid a "mechanical" sales vision. ### Transition to the 4 Cs * **Co-create:** Invite customers to help develop your next product. * **Currency:** Implement dynamic pricing strategies based on big data. * **Communal Activation:** Find ways to bring your product closer to the community for near-instant access. * **Conversation:** Encourage and respond to social media reviews, even the negative ones, to spark authentic dialogue. ### Adopt an H2H Mindset Ensure your CEO, CFO, and CMO are aligned on a humanistic view. This includes treating employees with empathy, as their satisfaction directly impacts the customer experience and the brand’s sustainability.

methodology: Philip Kotler (Chicago, 27 de maio de 1931) é um professor universitário estadunidense: Philip Kotler (Chicago, 27 de maio de 1931) é um professor universitário estadunidense. Distinto professor S.C. Johnson & Son de Marketing Internacional na Kellogg School of Management na Universidade Northwestern. Ele obteve seu mestrado na Universidade de Chicago e seu Ph.D. no Instituto Tecnológico de Massachusetts (MIT), ambos em economia. Ele fez pós-doutorado em matemática em Harvard e em ciências comportamentais na Universidade de Chicago. Foi selecionado em 2005 como o quarto maior guru de negócios pelo Financial Times (atrás de Jack Welch, Bill Gates e Peter Drucker) e foi considerado pelo Management Centre Europe "o maior dos especialistas na prática do marketing." Em 2008, o Wall Street Journal o listou como a sexta pessoa mais influente no mundo dos negócios. Kotler já prestou consultoria a grandes empresas, inclusive IBM, Michelin, Bank of America, Merck, General Electric, Honeywell e Motorola - nas áreas de estratégia de marketing, planejamento e organização e marketing internacional. Ele apresenta seminários em vários grandes centros ao redor do mundo sobre os últimos desenvolvimentos do marketing. Considerado um dos mercadólogos mais importante da história.

These negative voices often trigger "lovers" to defend the brand, creating a dynamic social dialogue. --- ## 2. The New Customer Journey: The 5 As The traditional AIDA (Attention, Interest, Desire, Action) funnel has been replaced by a more flexible, social model known as the **5 As**. This model accounts for the fact that buying is now a social process. | Stage | Description | Key Impression | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Assimilation (A1)** | Consumers are passively exposed to a long list of brands via ads or past experience. | "I know" | | **Attraction (A2)** | Consumers process messages and become attracted to a "shortlist" of memorable brands. | "I like" | | **Asking (A3)** | Driven by curiosity, consumers research, ask friends for advice, and check online reviews. | "I am convinced" | | **Action (A4)** | The consumer makes the purchase and interacts through consumption and post-sale service. | "I am buying" | | **Apology (A5)** | Consumers develop loyalty and spontaneously recommend or defend the brand to others. | "I recommend" | ### The Zone of Influence (POE) A consumer’s progress through the 5 As is influenced by the **Zone POE**: * **External (P):** Influence from brand communications (ads, sales teams). * **Others (O):** Influence from social circles, family, and online communities (the most powerful source). * **Personal (E):** Influence from the consumer’s own past experiences and preferences. --- ## 3. Redefining the Marketing Mix: From 4 Ps to 4 Cs In the digital economy, the traditional 4 Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion) must be re-envisioned to allow for greater customer participation. | Traditional 4 Ps | Digital 4 Cs | Strategic Definition | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Product** | **Co-creation** | Involving customers in the design and personalization of products. | | **Price** | **Currency** | Utilizing dynamic pricing that fluctuates based on market demand and data. | | **Place** | **Communal Activation** | Leveraging peer-to-peer distribution (e.g., Airbnb, Uber) and local access. | | **Promotion** | **Conversation** | Shifting from one-way broadcasting to two-way social dialogue and reviews. | *Note: Kotler also highlights an evolution to **7 Ps**, adding **Service**, **Brand**, and **Incentive** to the original mix to ensure competitiveness in a service-oriented market.* --- ## 4. Marketing H2H: Human-to-Human The latest evolution, **Marketing H2H**, proposes a movement to humanize marketing practices entirely. It focuses on empathy, ethics, and a sense of community. * **Design Thinking:** H2H incorporates design as a fundamental marketing tool for conceptualizing services and distribution. * **Service Integration:** It emphasizes services that accompany a product, focusing on the customer’s long-term needs (e.g., recycling printer cartridges rather than just selling new ones). * **Employee Centrality:** H2H recognizes that unhappy employees cannot provide a high-quality human experience to customers. --- ## 5. Important Quotes and Context > **"A brand is a promise; it works strongly and well if it fulfills what it promises, or fails if it doesn't."** *Context: Philip Kotler discussing the fundamental nature of branding in the 21st century.* > **"The company that relates to the customer only through technology is doomed to failure."** *Context: Kotler on the necessity of balancing "High-Tech" with "High-Touch" (human contact).* > **"Marketing H2H... is the new way of working in marketing: organizations so focused on their essence and role in the community that they can continuously adapt to their ecosystem's needs."** *Context: From the launch of Kotler’s "Marketing H2H" in Brazil, highlighting the need for strategic coherence.* > **"The ultimate goal of Marketing 4.0 is to drive consumers from assimilation to apology."** *Context: Explaining that the pinnacle of marketing success is no longer the sale, but creating a brand advocate.* --- ## 6. Actionable Insights ### Foster Brand Advocacy Do not just aim for a transaction. Map the customer journey to identify the "UAU!" (WOW!) moments that can turn a buyer into a defender. Focus on the **Apology** (Advocacy) stage as the primary metric of success. ### Engage the JMN Segments * **For Youth:** Use creative content and digital trends to gain "mind share." * **For Women:** Provide detailed, holistic information and quality comparisons to win "market share." * **For Netizens:** Facilitate online communities and authentic storytelling to win "heart share." ### Balance Tech and Touch Use Artificial Intelligence (IA) and data science to improve productivity and speed, but ensure these tools are partners to human empathy. AI can generate images and text, but it must be combined with human contact to avoid a "mechanical" sales vision. ### Transition to the 4 Cs * **Co-create:** Invite customers to help develop your next product. * **Currency:** Implement dynamic pricing strategies based on big data. * **Communal Activation:** Find ways to bring your product closer to the community for near-instant access. * **Conversation:** Encourage and respond to social media reviews, even the negative ones, to spark authentic dialogue. ### Adopt an H2H Mindset Ensure your CEO, CFO, and CMO are aligned on a humanistic view. This includes treating employees with empathy, as their satisfaction directly impacts the customer experience and the brand’s sustainability.
